mission.title: Create, verify, schedule, and restore etcd backups
mission.description: Operational runbook for protecting Kubernetes control-plane state with repeatable etcd snapshots and tested restore steps.

steps.title: Create an etcd snapshot from a control-plane node
steps.description:
Use `etcdctl` with the live API server certificates on a control-plane node and store the snapshot on durable disk.
```bash
export ETCDCTL_API=3
sudo mkdir -p /var/backups/etcd
sudo etcdctl \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  snapshot save /var/backups/etcd/etcd-snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db
```
If your cluster uses a stacked control plane, run this on one healthy control-plane node and copy the resulting snapshot to off-node storage.

steps.title: Verify the snapshot is usable before relying on it
steps.description:
Inspect the snapshot metadata and checksum immediately after creation.
```bash
LATEST_SNAPSHOT=$(ls -1t /var/backups/etcd/etcd-snapshot-*.db | head -1)
sudo etcdctl snapshot status "$LATEST_SNAPSHOT" --write-out=table
sha256sum "$LATEST_SNAPSHOT"
```
Record the revision, size, and checksum in your ops log so you can confirm you restore the intended backup later.

steps.title: Schedule periodic etcd backups with a CronJob
steps.description:
Automate snapshots on a secured administrative node or maintenance cluster using a CronJob that mounts the etcd certificates and a persistent backup volume.
```bash
kubectl create namespace etcd-backup
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: etcd-snapshot
  namespace: etcd-backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: etcdctl
            image: bitnami/etcd:3.5.14
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - >-
              ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl
              --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379
              --cacert=/certs/ca.crt
              --cert=/certs/server.crt
              --key=/certs/server.key
              snapshot save /backup/etcd-snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db
EOF
```
Back the CronJob with secure storage and retention policies; in production, also copy snapshots to object storage or another fault domain.
commands:
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: etcd-snapshot
  namespace: etcd-backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: etcdctl
            image: bitnami/etcd:3.5.14
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - >-
              ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl
              --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379
              --cacert=/certs/ca.crt
              --cert=/certs/server.crt
              --key=/certs/server.key
              snapshot save /backup/etcd-snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db
EOF

steps.title: Restore the cluster from a known-good snapshot
steps.description:
Stop the control-plane static Pods, restore the snapshot into a clean data directory, then point the etcd manifest at the restored data path. Validate API health before rejoining other control-plane nodes.
```bash
export ETCDCTL_API=3
sudo systemctl stop kubelet
sudo mv /var/lib/etcd /var/lib/etcd.broken-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
sudo etcdctl snapshot restore /var/backups/etcd/etcd-snapshot-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restore
sudo sed -i "s#--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd#--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restore#" /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
sudo systemctl start kubelet
kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose'
```
After the API server is healthy, restore the original static pod manifest path conventions that match your environment and reconcile any additional control-plane members.

resolution.summary: A verified etcd backup workflow is in place, scheduled backups are automated, and the restore procedure is documented for disaster recovery.
